group.title: Delivery timetable and the cause of delay

proposition_text: The go-live date was a target, not a fixed date, and was contractually moved to 18 November 2024 by Change Order No. 3, a Meridian-requested change that also added a customer loyalty module not in the original SOW scope (signed by Priya Nair on 2 September 2024).
summary: This denial is strongly and directly supported by the documentary and witness evidence, and it dismantles the late-delivery and no-scope-change allegations at once. The contractual framework treats the go-live date as a target rather than a fixed deadline: the Master Services Agreement (doc_003) provides for the SOW go-live date as a planning estimate adjustable through signed change control, and SOW-01 (doc_004) sets 1 October 2024 only as an original target while expressly excluding a customer loyalty module from scope. Deed of Variation No. 1 (doc_006) confirms that, as at late June 2024, scope and the 'target go-live date' were still unchanged, leaving Change Order No. 3 as the instrument that later varied them. Change Order No. 3 (doc_007), dated and signed 2 September 2024 by Priya Nair for Meridian, adds the loyalty module 'At the Customer's request', records that it 'was not part of the original scope under SOW-01', and revises 'the go-live date is revised from 1 October 2024 to 18 November 2024' — establishing every element of the denial. The contemporaneous emails (doc_010) show Meridian requesting the module in August 2024 and the parties agreeing to move go-live to 18 November via Change Order 3, and three Meridian witnesses corroborate each element: Helena Vance (doc_016), Priya Nair the signatory (doc_018), and TechFlow's solicitors' letter (doc_015); Raymond Okafor (doc_017) and the go-live email (doc_009) confirm 18 November as the operative date. The only adverse material is the Particulars of Claim (doc_002), which pleads (para 6) that the date 'was fixed at 1 October 2024, and that time was of the essence' and (para 9) that Meridian 'did not at any time request any change to the agreed scope'; but that untested pleading is directly contradicted by Meridian's own signed Change Order and its own witnesses, so it is only somewhat adverse rather than decisive. Confidence is very high given the signed contractual instrument and multi-source corroboration.

evidence.reasoning: The Claim Form gives only brief details of the claim for breach of contract, negligence and misrepresentation arising from defective and delayed implementation under the MSA. It does not say whether the go-live date was a target or fixed, and does not mention Change Order No. 3, the loyalty module, the 18 November 2024 revision or the SOW scope, so it does not address this denial.

This is the pleading the denial answers. The Particulars of Claim are adverse on both elements: para 6 pleads the go-live date 'was fixed at 1 October 2024, and that time was of the essence' (contradicting the denial's 'target, not a fixed date'), and para 9 pleads that 'Meridian did not at any time request any change to the agreed scope of the works' (contradicting the denial's 'Meridian-requested change' that added the loyalty module). However, this is an untested pleaded allegation that is directly contradicted by Meridian's own signed Change Order No. 3 (doc_007) and by its own witnesses (doc_016, doc_018), as well as the contractual target-date language (doc_003, doc_004, doc_006), so it is only somewhat adverse rather than decisive.
evidence.excerpt: Meridian did not at any time request any change to the agreed scope of the works. All delay was caused by TechFlow’s failure to allocate adequate and competent resources to the project.

evidence.reasoning: The Master Services Agreement supports the denial against the Particulars of Claim's 'fixed date' plea (doc_002, para 6). Clause 3.1 treats the SOW go-live date as a target/planning estimate rather than an immovable deadline, and clause 3.2 provides that any change to scope, deliverables or timetable is agreed by a signed Change Order which 'varies this Agreement to the extent stated in it'. This establishes both that the date was a target and the contractual mechanism by which Change Order No. 3 could lawfully revise it, though the MSA itself does not mention CO3 or the loyalty module.
evidence.excerpt: Any change to the scope, deliverables or timetable shall be agreed in writing by both parties by means of a signed Change Order. A signed Change Order varies this Agreement to the extent stated in it.

evidence.reasoning: SOW-01 establishes the original baseline that the denial says Change Order No. 3 varied, and rebuts both elements of the Particulars of Claim's plea (doc_002). It sets the original go-live as a target of 1 October 2024 (Milestone M4) and expressly states that the agreed scope 'does not include a customer loyalty module'. This corroborates that the date was an original target rather than a fixed deadline and that the loyalty module was outside the original SOW scope, directly supporting two elements of the denial.
evidence.excerpt: The Supplier shall deliver the Platform in two phases: Phase 1 (core inventory and EPOS); Phase 2 (reporting and analytics). The agreed scope does not include a customer loyalty module.

evidence.reasoning: The Phase 1 Order Form sets out the line items and charges (£2.4m total) under the MSA. It says nothing about whether the go-live date was a target or fixed, and does not mention Change Order No. 3, the loyalty module or any scope change, so it does not address this denial.

Deed of Variation No. 1 (28 June 2024) varied only the payment milestone profile and expressly recites that it 'does not vary the scope of the works or the target go-live date'. Its use of the phrase 'target go-live date' corroborates the denial's characterisation of the date as a target, and it confirms that, as at late June 2024, scope and the target date were still unchanged — leaving Change Order No. 3 (September 2024) as the instrument that later revised the date to 18 November and added the loyalty module. This mildly supports the denial's chronology and the 1 October 2024 starting point.
evidence.excerpt: This Deed does not vary the scope of the works or the target go-live date.

evidence.reasoning: This is Change Order No. 3 itself and is the primary direct evidence for the denial, decisively answering the Particulars of Claim (doc_002). It is dated and signed 2 September 2024 (by Priya Nair for the Customer and Marcus Reilly for the Supplier), adds the customer loyalty module 'At the Customer's request', records that the module 'was not part of the original scope under SOW-01', and provides that the go-live date 'is revised from 1 October 2024 to 18 November 2024'. It establishes every element of the denial: a Meridian-requested change, a loyalty module outside the original SOW scope, a contractual revision of the date to 18 November, and Priya Nair's signature on 2 September 2024.
evidence.excerpt: At the Customer’s request, the Supplier shall design, build and implement a customer loyalty module ("the Loyalty Module")... The Loyalty Module was not part of the original scope under SOW-01... The parties agree that, to accommodate the additional works, the go-live date is revised from 1 October 2024 to 18 November 2024.

evidence.reasoning: The Phase 1 UAT Acceptance Certificate (12 November 2024) confirms Phase 1 passed user acceptance testing. It concerns acceptance and outstanding snags, not whether the go-live date was a target, the revision to 18 November, the loyalty module or the SOW scope, so it does not address this denial.

This go-live decision email thread (24 October 2024) is mildly corroborative of the denial. It refers to 'the loyalty module added under Change Order 3' and treats 18 November 2024 as the scheduled go-live date Meridian instructed TechFlow to proceed with. This confirms both the existence of Change Order 3 adding the loyalty module and that 18 November was the operative revised go-live date, supporting the denial's facts (though its main focus is the go-live timing decision).
evidence.excerpt: the loyalty module added under Change Order 3 is still being hardened

evidence.reasoning: This email thread is direct contemporaneous support for the denial and rebuts the Particulars of Claim's no-scope-change plea (doc_002, para 9). On 21 August 2024 Helena Vance of Meridian requests a customer loyalty capability, acknowledging it was not in the original SOW and that Meridian was willing to discuss a revised date. On 27 August 2024 Marcus Reilly replies that the additional cost is £180,000, that 'we will need to move go-live from 1 October to 18 November 2024', and that he will issue Change Order 3 for Priya's signature 'to confirm both the scope and the revised date'. This corroborates that Meridian requested the module, it was outside the original scope, and the date moved to 18 November via Change Order 3.
evidence.excerpt: We’ve scoped the loyalty module. The additional cost is £180,000 and, given the additional build and test, we will need to move go-live from 1 October to 18 November 2024. I’ll issue Change Order 3 for Priya’s signature to confirm both the scope and the revised date.

evidence.reasoning: This email concerns the root cause of the 25 November 2024 outage (a Northgate Telecom network failure, not the Platform). It does not address whether the go-live date was a target, the revision to 18 November, Change Order No. 3, the loyalty module or the SOW scope, so it does not address this denial.

This internal Meridian email addresses causes of poor December 2024 trading (Lutterworth distribution centre flood, weak market, stock-sync issues). It does not address the target/fixed nature of the go-live date, Change Order No. 3, the loyalty module or the SOW scope, so it does not address this denial.

The defect/issue log covers post-go-live defects from 18 November 2024 onwards and their causes. While it references loyalty redemptions in one Sev 3 item (D-004), it does not address whether the go-live date was a target, the revision effected by Change Order No. 3, or whether the loyalty module was within the original SOW scope, so it does not address this denial.

The Notice of Termination/letter before action asserts repudiatory breach including that TechFlow 'delivered the Platform late' and refers to 'go-live on 18 November 2024'. It thus acknowledges 18 November 2024 as the go-live date (consistent with the denial) but says nothing about whether that date was a target, about Change Order No. 3, the loyalty module or the cause of the date moving, and instead frames the date as late delivery. On balance it neither materially supports nor contradicts this denial, so it is neutral.
evidence.excerpt: you delivered the Platform late; (ii) following go-live on 18 November 2024 the Platform suffered serious and repeated failures

evidence.reasoning: TechFlow's solicitors' response letter (7 February 2025) directly asserts the denial. It states that the go-live date 'moved from 1 October to 18 November 2024 by reason of Change Order No. 3, signed by your client on 2 September 2024, which added a customer loyalty module that your client requested and which was not in the original scope', and that Meridian's assertion of requesting no scope change 'is contradicted by its own signed Change Order'. This squarely supports the contractual revision of the date, the Meridian request, the out-of-scope loyalty module and Priya Nair's 2 September 2024 signature, while answering the Particulars of Claim (doc_002).
evidence.excerpt: It moved from 1 October to 18 November 2024 by reason of Change Order No. 3, signed by your client on 2 September 2024, which added a customer loyalty module that your client requested and which was not in the original scope.

evidence.reasoning: Helena Vance's witness statement strongly supports the denial from the requester's own evidence. As Meridian's Programme Director she states that in around August 2024 Meridian decided it wanted a loyalty capability, she 'asked TechFlow to add a loyalty module', accepts 'this was not in the original Statement of Work', and that it 'was documented in Change Order No. 3, which my colleague Priya Nair signed on 2 September 2024, and which also moved the go-live date to 18 November 2024'. This corroborates the Meridian request, the out-of-scope module, the 2 September 2024 signature and the move to 18 November, contradicting the Particulars of Claim (doc_002).
evidence.excerpt: I asked TechFlow to add a loyalty module. I accept this was not in the original Statement of Work. It was documented in Change Order No. 3, which my colleague Priya Nair signed on 2 September 2024, and which also moved the go-live date to 18 November 2024.

evidence.reasoning: Raymond Okafor's witness statement confirms that 'The new platform went live on 18 November 2024', corroborating the revised go-live date in the denial. He does not address whether the date was a target, Change Order No. 3, the loyalty module or the original SOW scope, so he supports only one element (the 18 November date), making this somewhat supportive.
evidence.excerpt: The new platform went live on 18 November 2024, immediately before our peak trading period.

evidence.reasoning: Priya Nair's witness statement is direct support from the signatory of Change Order No. 3 and confirms that Meridian understood it was changing both scope and date. She states that on 2 September 2024 she signed Change Order No. 3, which 'added the customer loyalty module, which Helena Vance had asked TechFlow to provide and which was not in the original Statement of Work, and it revised the go-live date from 1 October to 18 November 2024'. This corroborates every element of the denial — the Meridian request, the out-of-scope module, her 2 September 2024 signature and the contractual revision to 18 November — directly answering the Particulars of Claim (doc_002).
evidence.excerpt: On 2 September 2024 I signed Change Order No. 3. This added the customer loyalty module, which Helena Vance had asked TechFlow to provide and which was not in the original Statement of Work, and it revised the go-live date from 1 October to 18 November 2024.

evidence.reasoning: Dr Whitfield's IT expert report addresses Platform availability and the existence/severity of stock-synchronisation defects following go-live. It does not address whether the go-live date was a target, Change Order No. 3, the loyalty module, the revision to 18 November or the original SOW scope, so it does not address this denial.

Ms Greenhalgh's quantum expert report addresses wasted expenditure, loss of profit and causation of the Q4 shortfall. It does not address whether the go-live date was a target, Change Order No. 3, the loyalty module, the revision to 18 November or the original SOW scope, so it does not address this denial.
